EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system off the coast moved across Oregon and Washington with showers and thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms started off in central Oregon and tracked northward through the day.  Good dynamics with the low and associated jet allowed for several strong to severe thunderstorms to develop.  This in turn would become a line of thunderstorms with damaging outflow winds by the time it reached the Lower Columbia Basin.  Visibility was reduced to a quarter of a mile in several places due to blowing dust.  Highest wind gust reported was 63 MPH in Long Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, August 29, 2014, Oregon Department of Forestry Incident Management Team 2 (Incident Commander Chris Cline) was deployed to the Lost Hubcap Fire, burning approximately five miles south of the community of Monument. The Lost Hubcap Fire, reported around 1:30 p.m. on August 29th, quickly burned on Bureau of Land Management and private forestlands protected by ODF. The team assumed command of the fire from the local ODF unit of the Central Oregon District at 1200 hours PDT on Saturday, August 30, 2014. ||On Thursday, September 4, 2014, the Lost Hubcap Fire was declared 100 percent contained and ODF IMT 2 returned management of the fire to the local ODF John Day Unit of the Central Oregon District at 1700 hours. A local fire team will continue work on this fire, finishing any needed mop-up, as well as patrolling and monitoring it through the end of fire season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ochoco Complex consists of four wildfires on the Ochoco National Forest, and the Oscar Canyon fire burning on Prineville BLM land within the North Fork Wilderness Study Area. All fires are believed to have started by lightning on Sunday, July 13. ||The Lava fire, is located near the junction of Forest Road 22 and Forest Road 4210, near the northwest corner of Big Summit Prairie. It is currently estimated at 137 acres, 100% Contained. ||The Antelope fire, is located near Antelope Spring, about 3 miles east of Forest Road 22 along Forest Road 900, near the forest's northern border. It is currently estimated at 14 acres, 100% Contained. ||The Broadway fire, is located south of Forest Road 2630, near Peterson Lava. It is currently estimated at 5 acres, 100% Contained. ||The Fox/Oscar Canyon Fire, is located approximately 3 miles northwest of the North Fork Wilderness Study Area, near Forest Road 4230 and 17 miles east of Post, Oregon. It is currently estimated at 9,848 acres,94% contained.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire started just south of the town of Imnaha Oregon on August 3rd.  The cause of the wildfire is unknown. The 5 Mile Fire, located 20 miles northeast of Enterprise in the Imnaha River drainage, is currently over 4,000 acres in size, burning in steep terrain in grass and timber. It is located approximately 2 miles south of the town of Imnaha between the Imnaha River to the west, Hat Point Road to the East, High Camp Creek to the North, and Rippleton Creek to the South. The fire spread to 4,524 acres and was 95% contained as of August 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

Oregon Incident Management Team #1 (Oregon Team 1) is turning management of the Shaniko Butte Fire over to a Type 3 Team headed by Arlando Leyva at 6:00 a.m. Saturday morning. |The fire is 90% contained, and there are no visible smokes within 300 feet or more inside of the fire perimeter. Any hot spots that remain are well within the interior of the fire. |The Type 3 Team has 206 personnel assigned to manage final mop-up and rehab of the fire area. Helicopters are available if needed from Warm Springs Fire Management and the Logging Unit Complex helibase in Madras. |The Temporary Flight Restriction over the fire area has been lifted.|As interest in the fire wanes, along with the smoke and flame, the fire website, http://inciweb.nwcg.gov/incident/3960/ , will no longer be maintained by the Team, nor will updates be posted around the community or via e-mail.|Oregon Team 1 would like to thank the people of the Warm Springs area for their support and encouragement during the Shaniko Butte Fire. The appreciation expressed by the people in and around Warm Springs helped boost firefighter morale, and the hospitality of the community made the team feel welcome. While we are saddened by the loss of Hermie Boyd���s home on the day before we arrived, we are grateful that no additional homes were lost in spite of quick fire spread and the proximity of many homes and other structures. The successful suppression of the Shaniko Butte Fire is due in large part to the teamwork of many agencies and to the support of the people in the Warm Springs area. Thank you.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: InciWeb information.  One home destroyed in the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning moved through Central Oregon on July 11th and 12th sparking multiple fires on the Oregon Department of Forestry John Day Unit. 16 fires were staffed with three fires creating containment issues due to dry fuels and steep terrain. The Unit was out of firefighting resources therefore assistance was requested for additional resources including a management team to coordinate. Additional resources are arriving along with the Oregon Inter-agency Incident Management Team 3 (Team), Incident Commander Doug Johnson. The Team arrived on July 15th and assumed command of the fire fighting resources at 6 pm.  Multi-agency coordination. Multiple trail-heads, campgrounds, and an organizational camp evacuated and closed. The Ochoco NF has a closure order in place for a large portion of the forest due to multiple incidents. Total burned area 12,520.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: InciWeb information.

An early cold snap hit the Pacific Northwest before moist Pacific air moved in and resulted in one of the earliest snow, sleet, and freezing rain events in northwestern Oregon. Sleet and freezing rain in particular created hazardous commutes for tens of thousands in the western and eastern suburbs of Portland. Farther south, 1/2 of freezing rain accumulated on trees in the coast range foothills outside of Corvallis and Dallas, Oregon. Upwards of a quarter of an inch of ice fell around Dallas, Oregon. Some snow fell, but accumulations were primarily restricted to the Cascade valleys and the central Columbia River Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of 5 to 8 inches of snow in 12 hours in the Upper Hood River Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 974 millibar surface low moved northward paralleling the coast around 80 miles offshore, and resulted in one of the strongest wind storms that Northwest Oregon had experienced in past several of years. The strongest winds occurred as high pressure filled in behind the low. In this event, the Willamette Valley was just as susceptible to the strong winds as the coast and coast range were.   Most of the injuries, property damage, and power outages occurred in the Willamette Valley, but broken and toppled trees were widespread from the coast to the base of the Cascade foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds resulted in several downed trees and power outages. One tree damaged the roof of a home in Dayton.  Peak wind gusts for the Aurora and McMinnville Airports measured at 61 mph, and wind a peak wind gust of 71 mph was measured at Chehalem Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 974 millibar surface low moved northward paralleling the coast around 80 miles offshore, and resulted in one of the strongest wind storms that Northwest Oregon had experienced in past several of years. Most of the injuries, property damage, and power outages occurred in the Willamette Valley, but broken and toppled trees were widespread from the coast to the base of the Cascade foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph were reported at the Eugene Airport, but there were reports of at least 3 large trees downed. One of these trees fell on top of a SUV in Albany. The strong winds also resulted in numerous power outages in the Corvallis area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air plunged south out of the Canadian Rockies and created a east wind and down-slope wind storm across Northwest Oregon. There was fairly widespread east winds of 30 to 50 mph even along the Oregon coast. Downed trees resulted in property damage, transportation delays, and injured a bicyclist.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds resulted in downed trees throughout the metro area. Downed trees were responsible for the shut down of several sections of the MAX line for several hours, and the closures of Cornelius Pass Road and Shattuck Road. One tree crashed the roof of a house in Oregon City and pinned the resident under a desk inside. A biker sustained serious injuries when a tree fell on him and a near by car. A 54 mph easterly wind gust at the Portland Airport was the strongest east wind recorded since 1989.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of a quarter to a halfo of an inch of accumulated ice in the Southern Willamette Valley. Numerous trees and large limbs were downed or snapped by the weight of the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds moved into the interior Pacific Northwest over the January 10-12th weekend.  Several reports of high wind gusts and damage were recorded with these winds.  Wind gusts in MPH were reported: (71) west southwest of grass valley, (62) northwest of Clarno, (62) west northwest of Adams, (62) west northwest of Ruggs, (60) around Bend, (59.5) east of Bend, (59) north of Joseph, (58) west southwest of Shaniko, (58) south of Mikkalo, and (58) east southeast of Hermiston.  In Deschutes County, Mid state electric cooperative reported that strong winds downed a tree that cut off power to 11,000 customers. In Pendleton a basketball goal was blown over and as well as a fence from a suburban home.  Winds downed a large tree and cased a power outage at a lodge in Suttle Lake.  In Wallowa a roof peeled off of a house was reported on our social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty strong winds resulted in several downed trees for the Portland Metro Area. There were widespread road closures and power outages due to the downed trees. A Parapet of a Portland apartment in NW Portland fell and caused major damage to the apartment building. At least one power pole was downed by the winds in Washington County. A large tree fell onto a moving car injuring a mother and killing her son.

Wind gusts in MPH were reported: (71) west southwest of grass valley, (62) northwest of Clarno, (62) west northwest of Adams, (62) west northwest of Ruggs, (60) around Bend, (59.5) east of Bend, (59) north of Joseph, (58) west southwest of Shaniko, (58) south of Mikkalo, and (58) east southeast of Hermiston.  In Deschutes County, Mid state electric cooperative reported that strong winds downed a tree that cut off power to 11,000 customers. In Pendleton a basketball goal was blown over and as well as a fence from a suburban home.  Winds downed a large tree and cased a power outage at a lodge in Suttle Lake.  In Wallowa a roof peeled off of a house was reported on our social media.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front on 12/10/14 brought strong winds to many parts of southern Oregon and northern California.  A rapidly developing low pressure system behind the first front brought another round of high winds on 12/11/14.  Both of these events were covered by a long duration High Wind Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calimus RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval. The peak gusts of 76 mph were recorded at 11/0800 and 11/0900 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front on 12/10/14 brought strong winds to many parts of southern Oregon and northern California.  A rapidly developing low pressure system behind the first front brought another round of high winds on 12/11/14.  Both of these events were covered by a long duration High Wind Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summit RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 11/1403 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to portions of southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 66 mph, recorded at 20/1338 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sustained period of heavy precipitation brought flooding to a number of small and medium streams in Coos County in southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter at Sitkum reported that the East Fork of the Coquille River was flooded at its junction with Brummit Creek overflowed its banks at around 20/2215 PST.  A spotter 2NNW Sitkum reported that Brummit Creek was over its banks near where it empties into the East Fork of the Coquille River.   Flooding started at around 21/0225 PST.  At 21/0932 PST, the spotter at Sitkum reported water has reached a gymnasium structure and small amounts of water were on the floor.  The Sitkum-County Line road was under about 2 feet of water east of the bridge over Brummit Creek.  At the time of the report the water was beginning to recede.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving through the Great Basin spread snow and pockets of freezing rain across parts of Southwest Idaho and Southeast Oregon making roads slick and hazardous. Numerous accidents were reported including 3 fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to Oregon State Police, the driver of a semi-truck lost control of the vehicle and went over an embankment into a guard rail on Interstate 84 at mile marker 342 near Lime, Oregon. The driver was pronounced dead at the scene of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought strong winds to a number of locations along the Oregon coast and east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Blanco observations are not normally used to verify High Wind Warnings, but the sensor there recorded many gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 70 mph, recorded at 14/1245 PST just before an apparent power outage resulted in a lost of data.  Based on this gust of 70 mph, it is assumed that it gusted to 60 mph in some locations away from the cape.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought strong winds to a number of locations along the Oregon coast and east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ODOT sensor on Highway 101 at Humbug Mountain recorded a gust to 62 mph at 21/1823 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought strong winds to a number of locations along the Oregon coast and east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 63 mph, reported at 21/1638 and 21/1838 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought flooding to several rivers in southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Umpqua River at Elkton exceeded the flood stage of 33.0 feet at 21/2045 PST, crested at 34.16 feet at 22/0115 PST, then dropped below flood stage at 22/0530 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved into southwest Oregon, bringing sub-freezing temperatures to most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Bend airport reported a temperature of 32 degrees during most of this interval.  Other stations in Coos Bay recorded temperatures as low as 30 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into the Intermountain West on the 29th 0f the month spreading freezing rain, with up to a half of an inch of ice accumulation, and high mountain snow across parts of Eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Baker County Sheriff's office reported a quarter to a half of an inch of ice was covering most of the county. Numerous accidents were reported and Interstate 84 was closed between Baker and Huntington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Front resulted in gusty winds for the beaches and headlands of the Northwest Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two weather stations on the North Oregon Coast measured wind gusts of 63 mph, one at Garibaldhi and the other in Pacific City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Front resulted in gusty winds for the beaches and headlands of the Northwest Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six weather stations on the Central Oregon Coast measured wind gusts greater than 58 mph for at least 2 hours. The highest gust of 82 mph was recorded at Sea Lions Caves. The next highest gust, 70 mph, was measured in Newport at the Yaquina Bar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front on 12/10/14 brought strong winds to many parts of southern Oregon and northern California.  A rapidly developing low pressure system behind the first front brought another round of high winds on 12/11/14.  Both of these events were covered by a long duration High Wind Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Bank RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval, mostly between 11/0511 and 11/0911 PST.  The peak gust was 75 mph, recorded at 11/0611 PST.  The Signal Tree RAWS recorded a gust to 68 mph at 11/0835 PST.

An avalanche killed two people and seriously injured two others near Cornucopia Mountain in northern Baker County, Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people in a party of eight were caught in an avalanche about ten miles north of Halfway, Oregon or near Cornucopia around noon PST. Two are deceased, a guide  and a client, and are awaiting recovery. Two were seriously injured: one female with 2 broken legs and one broken arm, one male with a broken leg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm spread heavy snow across parts of Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho on the 7th of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported five to eight inches of snow across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm spread heavy snow across parts of Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho on the 7th of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported five to six inches of snow across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought a burst of strong winds to the Northwest Oregon Coast and Coast range. Some trees were downed resulting in power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two weather stations, Garibaldi and Clatsop Spit, reported sustained wind speeds around 42 mph on December 10th with a peak wind gust of 70 mph measured at Garibaldi. The Oregon Department of Transportation reported that the winds knocked a tree over onto power lines on US Highway 26 near milepost 3 resulting in a partial closure of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought a burst of strong winds to the Northwest Oregon Coast and Coast range. Some trees were downed resulting in power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather stations reported wind gusts greater than 62 mph on December 10th with the highest wind gust of 72 mph reported at Yaquina Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought a burst of strong winds to the Northwest Oregon Coast and Coast range. Some trees were downed resulting in power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oregon Department of Transportation reported that strong winds downed a tree onto power lines on US Highway 26 near milepost 3. This downed tree resulted in a partial closure of the highway.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL COAST RANGE OF W OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought a burst of strong winds to the Northwest Oregon Coast and Coast range. Some trees were downed resulting in power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bonneville Power Administration weather station at Mary's Peak measured sustained winds around 50 mph with frequent gusts to 75 mph for several hours on December 10th. The peak gust at this station was measured at 93 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and windy front moved onshore this day, bringing high winds to portions of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flynn Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 64 mph at 07/1613 PST and a gust to 65 mph at 07/1713 PST.  The ODOT sensor at Humbug Mountain recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph between 07/1548 PST and and 07/1818 PST.  The highest gust was 72 mph, recorded at 07/1603 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front resulted in strong winds for the Central Oregon Coast and the North Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three weather stations along the Central Oregon Coast reported strong gusty winds in excess of 56 mph. The strongest gust was 62 mph measured at the Yaquina Bridge in Newport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought strong winds to the Oregon coast as well as South Central Oregon east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Blanco winds are not normally used to verify High Wind Warnings.  However, Cape Blanco did record several gusts exceeding 70 mph during this interval, included a gust to 76 mph at 12/0015 PST.  Given these very high gusts, it is assumed that High Wind Warning criteria were met at other nearby locations away from the Cape.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought strong winds to the Oregon coast as well as South Central Oregon east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded gusts meeting warning criteria continuously from 11/2238 PST until 12/0438 PST.  The strongest gust was 65 mph, recorded at 12/0038 PST.   The Rock Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 12/0127 PST.  The Summit RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 12/0703 PST.  A member of the public 3NE Valley Falls recorded a gust to 60 mph at 12/0259 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused the Coquille River at Coquille to flood. The flood was categorized as a moderate flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille exceeded the flood stage of 21.0 feet at 15/0245 PST, then exceeded the moderate flood stage of 23.0 feet at 15/1800 PST.  It crested at 23.40 feet several times between 16/0415 PST and 16/0915 PST.  The river fell below moderate flood stage at 17/0415 PST and below flood stage at 18/0915 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused a brief flood on Little Butte Creek at Eagle Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Butte Creek at Eagle Point exceeded the flood stage of 10.0 feet at 14/1500 PST.  It crested at 11.95 feet at 14/2015 PST.  The creek fell below flood stage at 14/2345 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front resulted in strong winds for the Central Oregon Coast and the North Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near the Timberline Lodge measured a wind gust of 121 mph with sustained winds of 92 mph. This station reported nine consecutive hours of wind greater than 100 mph. Santiam pass which usually is conservative on wind reports recorded a peak wind gust of 67 mph. The chair lifts at Mount Hood Meadows were closed due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air remained trapped in the Upper hood River Valley area as a front moved in and produced freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various of stations reported 0.25 to 0.4 inch of ice accumulation including Parkdale and Middle Mountain. A newspaper article reported a fatal car crash due to ice on Highway 26 in Wasco County, just northeast of the upper hood river valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry arctic cold front brought very cold air and windy conditions to the North Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind and temperatures measurements for Mount Hood Meadows resulted in a wind chill of negative 26 degrees Celsius.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty East winds moving through the Columbia River Gorge resulted in damaging winds for the Portland Metro Area. Measured wind gusts of 30 mph were enough to knock several large trees down resulting in property damage, power outages, and transportation delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East winds blowing through the gorge were strong enough to knock over trees in the Portland Metro area resulting in minor damage. One tree in Southwest Portland came down onto a house. Another tree knocked down near the Sellwood Bridge resulted in the closing of Highway 43 and caused major traffic delays. Several trees were knocked down in Gresham with at least one falling down on power lines and causing power outages. Another tree in Gresham fell onto a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to several areas around southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Squaw Peak RAWS recorded several gusts exceeded 74 mph between 15/0939 PST and 15/1239 PST.  The peak gust was 79 mph, recorded at 15/1139 PST/.  The Zim RAWS recorded two gusts exceeding 74 mph:  70 mph at 15/1039 PST and 83 mph at 15/1139 PST.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to several areas around southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Agrimet station at Beatty recorded a gust to 58 mph at 15/1415 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass brought freezing temperatures to portions of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 32 to 34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass brought freezing temperatures to portions of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 30 to 36 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boy was injured as a sneaker wave lifted a log and rolled it on top of him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sneaker wave surprised some children who jumped on a log to avoid getting wet. The wave lifted the log which then rolled onto a 10 year old boy. The boy was seriously injured including bone fractures, collapsed lungs and a lacerated liver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional snow and ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event in a series where there was a widespread snow/ice event on March 7th that followed a widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 3 inches of additional snow fell on March 8th before the precipitation changed to freezing rain in the afternoon. The freezing rain persisted through midnight. A NWS Employee reported 2.6 inches of snow followed by 0.1 inch of ice in Southwest Portland. Another NWS employee reported 2 inches of snow followed by a glaze of ice in SE Portland. A spotter in Clackamas reported 0.25 inch of ice and there were two reports in Gresham of 0.25 inch ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional snow and ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event in a series where there was a widespread snow/ice event on March 7th that followed a widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local media meteorologist reported 0.5 to 0.75 inch of ice in Corbett. Worst impacts were near Cascade Locks where numerous crashes occurred on Interstate 84 resulting in intermittent closures due to snow and ice. There was also several power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional snow and ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event in a series where there was a widespread snow/ice event on March 7th that followed a widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although there were not many reports in the Central Columbia River Gorge, the reports that follow from the Western Columbia Gorge are near enough to verify that snow and ice also occurred in the Central Gorge. A local media meteorologist reported 0.5 to 0.75 inch of ice in Corbett. The worst impacts were near Cascade Locks where numerous crashes occurred on Interstate 84 resulting in intermittent closures due to snow and ice. There was also several power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system brought a couple of fronts across the Pacific Northwest January 11 through January 12, and resulted in heavy snow for the Northern Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two USGS weather stations near Mount Hood reported heavy snow. One was at Timberline (TIM60) around 6000 feet where 21 inches was measured between 0600 PST January 11 to 1000 PST January 12. The 1 inch per hour criteria was met at 0600 PST (January 11th). The other station is at Mount Hood Measows (MHM52) at 5200 feet where 15 inches of snow was reported between 1900 PST on January 11th and 1000 PST January 12. The 1 inch per hour criteria was met here at the onset of the snow (January 11 at 1900 PST).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds help trap cold air into the Upper Hood River and Central Columbia River Gorge areas as a Pacific frontal system produced freezing rain with light ice accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain impacted the upper hood river valley area with two reports from the public of ice accumulations around 0.25 inch just northwest of Parkdale on Trout Creek Road (1500 feet elevation).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass brought freezing temperatures to portions of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 29 to 36 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds ripped through the Columbia River Gorge overnight (early morning) on January 10th.  Damage reported by the Dalles Chronicle include: a section of a roof blown off of a restaurant, a tree down on Mill Creek Rd. just south of The Dalles, and recycling materials and an old Christmas trees were scattered east of The Dalles.
A complex low pressure system brought a couple of fronts across the Pacific Northwest January 11 through January 12, and resulted in heavy snow for the Northern Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was determined for the Lane County Cascades by the Roaring River SNOTEL station (ROR03) at 4900 feet where 15 inches of snow was measured between 0800 PST January 11th through 0400 PST January 12th. The 1 inch per hour criteria was met at 1600 PST on January 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific Storm brought windy conditions to the North Oregon coast and Coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many weather stations along the North Oregon coast reported High Winds with the strongest winds reported at Megler bridge (ODT76) with sustained winds of 55 mph, and a peak gust of 79 mph. The high winds were reported from Tillamook to Astoria and average around 45 mph sustained with 70 mph gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific Storm brought windy conditions to the North Oregon coast and Coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many weather stations reported high winds along the Central Oregon Coast from Newport to Gleneden Beach. The highest gust was 76 mph reported at Gleneden Beach (C9821). Generally sustained winds of 40 mph with gusts around 60 mph were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific Storm brought windy conditions to the North Oregon coast and Coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tidewater RAWS (MTR03) measured Sustained winds of 46 mph with a peak gust of 72 mph.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL COAST RANGE OF W OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific Storm brought windy conditions to the North Oregon coast and Coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockhouse RAWS (RKH03) measured sustained winds of 47 mph with a peak gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge produced Gusty East winds that resulted in minor damage around the Portland Metro Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds resulted in knocking two large trees over. One of these trees came down on a SUV in Milwaukie, and the other came down on an SUV in southwest Portland. There were also 100s of people without power for at least an hour due to downed limbs on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought strong winds and heavy snow to portions of the southern Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cascade Summit SNOTEL recorded 14 inches of snow in 11 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought strong winds to portions of southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ODOT sensor at Humbug Mountain recorded numerous gusts of 58 mph or greater during this interval.  The peak gust was 80 mph, recorded at 05/1546 PST and 05/1601 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy long period west swell brought heavy surf to the Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Umpqua Offshore Buoy recorded swell that would support average breaker heights of up to 40 feet.  The Port Orford Buoy recorded swell that would support average breaker heights of up to 36 feet.  The Point Saint George buoy recorded swell that would support average breaker heights of up to 34 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy long period west swell brought heavy surf to the Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Umpqua Offshore Buoy recorded swell that would support average breaker heights of up to 40 feet.  The Port Orford Buoy recorded swell that would support average breaker heights of up to 36 feet.  The Point Saint George buoy recorded swell that would support average breaker heights of up to 34 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event after a widespread snow/ice event on the 7th, and a  widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Portland issued its first Wireless Emergency Alert in history on March 9th at 8 AM warning people to stay indoors due to dangerous ice conditions. While most of the icing has stopped by then, there was still significant ice loading on branches and power lines as well as several of each downed. Power outages continued into the 9th due to the ice, but ice slowly melted as temperatures crept above freezing. Some ice accumulations reported from spotters include a 0.30 inch in Gresham, and a 0.25 inch of ice in Clackamas. Public reports include 0.4 inch of ice in Fairview and 0.3 inch in Northeast Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event after a widespread snow/ice event on the 7th, and a  widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy icing led to widespread power outages in Lane County, leaving thousands without power. A picture on the Eugene Water and Electric's Facebook page showed some downed power lines with around 1 inch of ice accumulated on them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event after a widespread snow/ice event on the 7th, and a  widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public Utility's website reported that more than 3800 customers were without power in the Sweet Home area due to ice. Spotters reported 0.5 to 0.5 inch of ice accumulation in Salem and 0.25 inch in Dallas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought strong winds to portions of southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded a gust to 63 mph at 06/1238 PST and a gust to 65 mph at 06/1338 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought strong winds to portions of southern Oregon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming cold front brought high winds to portions of the Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ODOT sensor at Humbug Mountain recorded six gusts exceeding 57 mph during this inteval.  The peak gust was 63 mph, recorded at 28/1031 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event after a widespread snow/ice event on the 7th, and a  widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public Utility's website reported that more than 3800 customers were without power in the Sweet Home area due to ice. A Spotter reported 0.5 inch of ice accumulation in Sweet Home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass combined with strong easterly winds resulted in very cold wind chill values for the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the single digits combined with persistent 20 to 30 mph winds produced wind chills in the -15 to -20 degrees Fahrenheit range. Timberline Lodge reported the most extreme wind chill where temperatures were as low as -5 degrees and winds gusting to 25 mph produced a wind chill as low as -31 degrees at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy westerly swell brought high surf to the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Umpqua Offshore Buoy recorded swell heights supporting a Heavy Surf Warning during this interval.  The peak swell was  7.0 m (23 feet) with a period of 17 seconds, recorded at 19/2326 PST and 20/0056 PST.  This would support an average breaker height of 32 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Launch Wildfire started on 07/28/2014 at around 1200 PST.  It was human-caused.  As of 08/01/2014, the fire was held at 42 acres and was 60% contained.  325000 dollars were spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men were swept into the ocean by rip currents at different beaches in Seaside around 5:20 pm on July 22nd, 2014. One man was rescued and refused medical attention. The other man was unable to be revived after being pulled out of the ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Seaside Fire and Rescue received two seperate calls within 2 minutes of eachother to rescue a man that has been swept to sea by strong ocean currents in Seaside, OR. One man was pulled out of the ocean by 4 rescuers, and walked away with no known injuries. The other man was dragged 3 blocks south by the current, and did not survive. The seas were 3 feet around 11 seconds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low combined with monsoonal moisture resulted in widespread lightning over Northwest Oregon and Southwest Washington. The convection was mostly elevated but resulted in power outages and some damage to parts of the Central Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abundant lightning resulted in around 1000 PGE customers without power in the Albany and Corvallis area. A public caller reported that a large tree struck from lightning fell onto a home.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Oregon Department of Forestry's Incident Management Team 2 (Incident Commander Chris Cline) and the Oregon State Fire Marshal's Office Incident Management Team Green (Incident Commander John Ingrao) were dispatched to and assumed joint incident command at noon on Sunday, June 8, 2014, of the Two Bulls Fires. These two fires, burning towards each other when first reported (Saturday afternoon, June 7), are being managed as one incident, are located approximately 10 miles northwest of Bend, and burning in a mix of Deschutes National Forest and private lands protected by the Oregon Department of Forestry. The wind- and terrain-driven fires prompted immediate evacuations in areas west of Bend, as well as additional threats/evacuation notices. Evacuations and the threats to structures were downgraded within a few days, with the Oregon State Fire Marshal's Office Incident Management Team Green demobilized at the end of shift on June 10. On Saturday, June 14, 2014, responsibility for management of the Two Bulls Fire transitioned to a local Type 3 fire team. At the time of transition, no homes or structures had been damaged or lost as a result of this fire, however, the fire burned approximately 6,908 acres of public and private forestlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: InciWeb Report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist front produced heavy rain for the North Oregon Coast Range that resulted in brief flooding of the Nehalem River near Foss.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in the Nehalem River to flood near Foss. The river reached flood stage around 2 pm March 6, and crested at 14.8 feet at 8 pm. The river fell below flood stage around 9 am on March 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds brought very cold air from east of the Cascades through the Columbia River Gorge as a moist front pushed in from the Pacific. The combination of the cold air mass and frontal precipitation resulted in snow and ice for the Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of snow and ice in the Central Columbia River Gorge with generally 6 to 8 inches of snow. There was a quarter of an inch of ice on top of the snow in Hood River and White Salmon, and as much as 0.4 to 0.5 inch of ice in Parkdale where the cold air held on the longest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The 790 Wildfire was started by lightning in the Sky Lakes Wilderness Area on 07/31/2014.  Since it was in a wilderness area, it was allowed to burn until it reached National Forest land.   As of the last report on 09/26/2014, the fire covered 3023 acres and was 90% contained. 16.0 million dollars was spent on firefighting efforts. No structures were lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused on prescribed fire to lose control east of the Pendleton.  This fire would go on for several acres and burned mostly farm land, however there was a scrap yard that was overtaken by the fire.  Another fire start near Lexington was able to spread due to strong winds and destroyed a mobile home and a couple of vehicles, before fire crews were able to extinguish it.

On Friday, August 29, 2014, Oregon Department of Forestry Incident Management Team 2 (Incident Commander Chris Cline) was deployed to the Lost Hubcap Fire, burning approximately five miles south of the community of Monument. The Lost Hubcap Fire, reported around 1:30 p.m. on August 29th, quickly burned on Bureau of Land Management and private forestlands protected by ODF. The team assumed command of the fire from the local ODF unit of the Central Oregon District at 1200 hours PDT on Saturday, August 30, 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds brought very cold air from east of the Cascades through the Columbia River Gorge as a moist front pushed in from the Pacific. The combination of the cold air mass and frontal precipitation resulted in snow and ice for the Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 0.5 to 0.75 inch of ice accumulated in the Western Columbia River Gorge resulting in numerous reports of accidents due to icy roads on Interstate 84. A Spotter reported .25 inch in Stevenson and a local media meteorologist reported 0.75 inch in Corbett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire started just south of the town of Imnaha Oregon on August 3rd. The cause of the wildfire is unknown. The 5 Mile Fire, located 20 miles northeast of Enterprise in the Imnaha River drainage, is currently over 4,000 acres in size, burning in steep terrain in grass and timber. It is located approximately 2 miles south of the town of Imnaha between the Imnaha River to the west, Hat Point Road to the East, High Camp Creek to the North, and Rippleton Creek to the South. The fire spread to 4,524 acres and was 95% contained as of September 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a wild fire south of the John day area on August 2nd. The fire would go on to spread over 2500 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought flooding to several rivers in southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille exceeded the minor flood stage of 21.0 feet at 21/1230 PST and exceeded the moderate flood stage of 23.0 feet at 21/2030 PST. The river crested at 24.17 feet between 22/1030 and 22/1045 PST. The river dropped below moderate flood stage at 24/0015 PST, and dropped below minor flood stage at 26/1400 PST.

A series of fronts produced a period of rain that resulted in rock and land slides over burn scarred areas of the Clackamas National Forest near Estacada, Oregon. The burn scars were from the 36 pit wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oregon Department of Transportation reported land and mud slides near Estacada, Oregon after 2 inches of rain fell over the past week. These slides closed Higway 226 near milepost 36 for several of days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three people wading in the water near the north jetty in Tillamook were swept out to sea by a sneaker wave. Two of the people made it to shore safely, but one did not.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports that a large sneaker wave took three people wading in the water near the north jetty of Tillamook out to see. Two of the people made it back to shore safely, but one did not, and was presumed to have drowned.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep strong low pressure system off the coast provided strong southerly flow to the forecast area.  With a strong upper level jet and associated strong low level jet running perpendicular to the mountain terrain, and a strong surface low that set up over the Washington Basin would tighten the gradients along the Blue Mountain Foothills.  Wind reports in MPH are as followed: Along with multiple reports of damage the Northern Blue Mountain Foothills recorded 9 separate gusts ranging from (58-95), with the strongest gust 1 mile WNW of Cayuse.  8 separate gusts ranging from (59-88) along the Southern Blue Mountain Foothills, with the strongest gust 1 mile NW of Heppner.  A few gusts ranging from (68-75) near Joseph. A couple of gusts of (60) and (71) along the Northern Blue Mountains, with the strongest gust 3 miles WSW Kooskooskie.  A few gusts ranging from (63-70) across the Ochoco and John Day Highlands, with the strongest gust measured at the John Day Airport.  A couple of gusts of (67) and (69) across the Southern Blue Mountains, with the highest gust 5 miles NNE of Fox.  A gust of (60) across the Grand Ronde Valley in Ladd and Pyles Canyon.  A gust of (60) along with a couple reports of damage in Central Oregon, with the wind gust reported at the Redmond Airport.  Additionally there were a couple reports of several tree branches up to 6 inches in diameter across the John Day Valley, as well as a few damage reports with downed trees and power lines for the East Slopes of the Oregon Cascades.

A warm front from the south collided with an arctic air mass from the north and produced significant winter weather across most of central and northeast Oregon.  Heavy snow occurred across much of central and northeast Oregon, with the highest accumulations over central Oregon.  In addition to the heavy snow, portions of central Oregon reported between 0.50-1.00 of ice from freezing rain.  Snowfall amounts in inches are as followed: (21) NE of Sisters, (20) NNW of Redmond, (19) E of Prineville, (13) and 0.50 of ice N of Prineville, (8) ESE of Granite, (7) SE of Cove, (7) E of The Dalles, (6) and 1.00 of ice SE of Bend, (6) in Pilot Rock, (6) W of Lexington, (6) E of Union, (5) in John Day, and (4) in Echo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to the coast and some inland locations on this date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported wind gusts estimated at 50-60 mph downed several trees in the Dark Hollow area southwest of Medford.  The tops of two large healthy trees were broken, one an oak and the other a poplar.  No property damage.  The high winds lasted around 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to many parts of southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coffee Pot RAWS reported a gust to 59 mph at 25/0644 PST and a gust to 61 mph at 25/1344 PST.  The Rock Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 25/0727 PST.  The Summit RAWS recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph between 25/0603 and 25/1003 PST. The peak gust was 65 mph recorded at 25/1003 PST.  The ODOT sensor at Valley Falls recorded a gust to 60 mph at 25/0750 PST and a gust to 62 mph at 25/0800 PST. A spotter 9SSE Summer Lake reported a gust to 85 mph at 25/1655 PST with sustained winds 50-60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to many parts of southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flynn Prairie RAWS reported numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 25/0413 and 25/1013 PST.  The peak gust was 73 mph at 25/0613 and 25/0813  PST.  The Gold Beach METAR reported numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 25/0439 and 25/0759 PST. The peak gust was 62 mph recorded at 25/0619 PST. The Gold Beach ODOT sensor recorded a peak gust of 60 mph at 25/0903 PST. The ODOT Sensor at Humbug Mountain reported numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 25/0017 and 25/0132 PST and again from 25/0717 and 25/1117 PST. The peak gust was 92 mph recorded at 25/0832 PST. A spotter 1SE Brookings reported a gust of 63 mph at 25/0620 PST and a peak gust to 69 mph at 25/0645 PST.  A spotter 2NW Brookings reported several trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front brought high winds to many parts of southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Long Prairie RAWS reported a gust to 66 mph at 25/0913 PST.  The Port Orford NOS-NYLON reported numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 25/0536 and 25/0912 PST. The peak gust was 77 mph recorded at 25/0448 PST. The Port of Port Orford reported 100 mph winds and 28 foot waves, which damaged a jetty and caused extensive damage at the dock.  Numerous crab totes were lost, the Hallmark Fisheries office was destroyed, other offices were flooded. and boulder were tossed into the dock and parking lot. Damage was estimated at $1 million dollars  Trees toppled, some of which fell across trailers at the Madrona RV park.  One trailer caught fire, and the occupant was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front on 12/10/14 brought strong winds to many parts of southern Oregon and northern California.  A rapidly developing low pressure system behind the first front brought another round of high winds on 12/11/14.  Both of these events were covered by a long duration High Wind Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sensor DW6803 at Science Works in Ashland recorded sporadic high wind gusts during this interval, with a peak gust of 74 mph recorded at 11/0303 PST and a sustained wind of 15 mph.  The data from this source is suspect, but there were numerous reports of wind damage on this morning from around Jackson County, and there was one fatality when a tree fell on a camper's tent.  The Medford Mail Tribune reported that the roof was blown off of one Medford business and a smaller building collapsed.   A city-owned barn in Ashland lost part of its roof.  Numerous power lines were down and there were power failures affecting 16000 people.  Dozens of trees were felled, some of them falling on cars.  In rural Jackson county, a tree fell on a power line which in turn fell on a house, setting it on fire.  The structure was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convective showers produced strong winds that resulted in minor structural damage near Woodburn, OR.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A convective shower resulted in minor wind damage consistent with straight line wind around 2 miles west northwest from Woodburn, OR. Several 3 to 8 inch diameter tree limbs were downed along with some smaller debris. Two 10 inch diameter rotted trees snapped and fell onto a house with no structural damage. There was also minor damage to an irrigation pipe located at the intersection of Butterville Road and LeBrun Rd. Several fences were also reported to be damaged in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oregon Department of Transportation suggested that heavy rain was responsible for a small landslide that occurred near Corbett, Oregon. Official NWS stations reported between 0.5 to 3 inches of rain in Multnomah County in the afternoon and evening. The landslide closed both lanes of the Historic Columbia River Highway just east of the Vista House for a few hours Wednesday evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of people reported a cold core funnel cloud between Woodburg and Gervais, OR around 145 pm. Some damage was reported in this area around the same time, but a NWS storm survey determined the damage was more consistent with straight line wind damage than from a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 983mb low pressure system approached from the Southwest and moved north along the Central Oregon Coast before making landfall near Gray's Harbor, Washington. This low resulted in Gusty winds for the coast and coast range as well as damaging winds across the Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 60 mph were reported throughout the Portland Metro Area. There were numerous trees downed by these winds with some landing on power lines. There was at least two homes that had major damage due to downed trees. Portland General Electric reported around 140,000 people without power by the end of the storm. One downed power line burned a hole in the road that it fell on. A NWS employee reported that a large gust blew doors open at a SW Portland restaurant, and ripped large paintings off the wall. Some of the downed trees blocked roads, and 2 lanes were blocked on Highway 30 for a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cold air mass allowed temperatures to drop below freezing over many areas in southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 27 to 36 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cold air mass allowed temperatures to drop below freezing over many areas in southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 26 to 41 degrees.  Subfreezing temperatures appeared to be isolated, as only one station was below 34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cold air mass combined to bring subfreezing temperatures to parts of the Umpqua Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 22 to 36 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and a cold air mass combined to bring subfreezing temperatures to parts of the Oregon coast and Umpqua Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 24 to 40 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on Radar and nearby reports, 6 to 12 inches of snow were followed by 0.25 to 0.5 inch of ice, leading to numerous downed trees and power outages. The Emergency Manager for Hood River County reported 8 inches of snow in Parkdale along with 0.25 inch of ice. There were several reports from the Oregon Department of Transportation of Black Ice on roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on radar and nearby reports, up to 6 inches of snow were followed by 0.5 to 1 inch of ice leading to numerous downed trees and power outages. The Eugene Water and Electric reported numerous downed power lines and shared a picture on Facebook with around an inch of ice on one of their power lines in Oakridge. This was a widespread damaging event which covered Lane County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in nearby  Longview, WA reported 4 inches of snow followed by 0.1 to 0.25 inch of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A preceding cold arctic air mass combined with a moist Pacific storm resulted in widespread heavy snow for Northwest Oregon including the coast and the Willamette Valley. Warmer air quickly moved in from the south mid-morning and the snow changed to freezing rain.  East winds through the Columbia River Gorge maintained colder air to the north where the precipitation fell mainly as snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Central Willamette Valley was in between two heavier bands of snow to their north and to their south and generally had less snow than other areas in NW Oregon. However, a couple of locations still met the Winter Weather Warning criteria including 5 inches of snow 8 miles northwest of Newberg and 4 inches at Monmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A preceding cold arctic air mass combined with a moist Pacific storm resulted in widespread heavy snow for Northwest Oregon including the coast and the Willamette Valley. Warmer air quickly moved in from the south mid-morning and the snow changed to freezing rain.  East winds through the Columbia River Gorge maintained colder air to the north where the precipitation fell mainly as snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow came fast and furious into the South Willamette Valley and continued heavy at times throughout the day. Most locations saw 5 to 10 inches of snow. The highest measured snow amounts follow. Two trained spotters reported 11.5 inches of snow at Corvallis and 9.3 inches of snow at Philomath. A public report provided 9 inches in Albany, and an amateur HAM radio operator relayed 7 inches of snow in Halsey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A preceding cold arctic air mass combined with a moist Pacific storm resulted in widespread heavy snow for Northwest Oregon including the coast and the Willamette Valley. Warmer air quickly moved in from the south mid-morning and the snow changed to freezing rain.  East winds through the Columbia River Gorge maintained colder air to the north where the precipitation fell mainly as snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were no snow observations from within this zone, but heavy snow in near by Detroit (1500 feet elevation) confirms there was heavy snow in the Oregon Cascade Foothills. A trained spotter reported 14 inches of snow in Detroit and ODOT reported 9 inches of snow. Therefore, it is estimated that there was 7 to 15 inches of snow in the foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A preceding cold arctic air mass combined with a moist Pacific storm resulted in widespread heavy snow for Northwest Oregon including the coast and the Willamette Valley. Warmer air quickly moved in from the south mid-morning and the snow changed to freezing rain.  East winds through the Columbia River Gorge maintained colder air to the north where the precipitation fell mainly as snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hood River County Emergency Manager reported 10 inches of snow in Hood River. The Oregon Department of Transportation reported 9 inches in Hood River and 8 inches in Cascade Locks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Weather system brought additional ice to Northwest Oregon March 8th. This is the third cold weather event after a widespread snow/ice event on the 7th, and a  widespread heavy snow event on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy icing led to widespread power outages in Lane County, leaving thousands without power. A picture on the Eugene Water and Electric's Facebook page showed some downed power lines with around 1 inch of ice accumulated on them. A spotter in Lebanon reported 0.5 inch of ice and there were several reports of 0.3 to 0.75 inch ice in Eugene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to Northwest Washington March 5, 2014 during the evening. The associated deep surface low moved inland into Vancouver Island, WA early the next morning (March 6th) dragging a bent-back occluded front across Northwest Oregon that produced another round of strong winds. The winds were less strong in the Willamette Valley than the coastal areas, but were strong enough to result in  some significant wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front combined with a coastal barrier jet produced strong winds for the Northern and Central Oregon Coasts and Coast range March 8, 2014. The NWS Doppler Radar Velocity product detected 50 to 60 kt winds just offshore the coast between 1500 and 4000 feet above the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oregon Department of Transportation's weather station on Yaquina Bridge measured sustained winds of 45 mph for an hour in the afternoon on March 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across much of the northern Blue Mountains and Wallowa County throughout the first week of March.  March 9th received very heavy rain with snow levels around 6000ft.  This allowed for a significant increase in runoff, which lead to a quick rise in rivers for the period. Flooding was reported at the confluence of Five Points Creek and the Grande Ronde River near Hilgard.  Field flooding occurred along the Grande Ronde River from Island City downstream through Imber to Elgin.  Water encroached into the school grounds at Troy.  Along the Umatilla River, water was reported running beneath a structure near Cayuse, there was sandbagging in the Riverside area near Pendleton with water into several yards.  There continues to be bank erosion along the river from Reith to Echo.  The walking trail and picnic areas were flooded in Oxbow Park in Hermiston.  Along the John Day River, water rose onto the shoulders of Oregon highway 207 between Spray and Service Creek.  There was also small stream flooding along Meacham Creek and Wildhorse Creek,  with water spilling into pastures.
A strong cold front produced strong winds for the North Oregon coast and coast range on February 15, 2014. Highways 26 and 53 were closed due to downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather stations along the entire North Oregon coast measured high winds on February 15, 2014. The strongest wind gust was 86 mph which was measured at Garibaldi NOS (TLB03). Pacific City (AT297), Megler Bridge (ODT76), and Clatsop Spit (3CLO3) measured peak wind gusts between 69 and 72 mph. A spotter in Tillamook reported a wind gust of 65 mph, and the Astoria Airport measured a peak gust of 60 mph. Cannon Beach (D4535) reported a peak gust of 58 mph. Sustained winds were also high at Garibaldi (61 mph) and Megler Bridge (56 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced strong winds for the North Oregon coast and coast range on February 15, 2014. Highways 26 and 53 were closed due to downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds was reported at the Tidewater RAWS (MTR03) with sustained winds of 38 mph and a peak gust of 67 mph. Highways 26 and 53 were closed due to downed trees blocking the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a low level coastal jet produced strong winds for the North and Central Oregon coast and coast range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two stations in the Northwest Oregon Coast Range measured high winds. The Hebo BPA weather station reported 11 hours of high winds with a peak gust of 63 mph, and the Tidewater RAWS (MTRO3) measured sustained winds of 35 mph with a peak gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to Northwest Washington March 5, 2014 during the evening. The winds were less strong in the Willamette Valley than the coastal areas, but were strong enough to result in  some significant wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds resulted in significant damage in the Portland Metro Area with area wind measurements between 30 and 48 mph. There were several reports of 12 to 24 inch diameter trees down. One landed on the Firestone Tire Building downtown Portland, one closed a busy intersection at Hall and Bonita in Tigard, and another closed 'A' Avenue downtown Lake Oswego. There were numerous power outages where tens of thousands of PGE customers were without power. Clackamas Community College cancelled all classes on March 6 due to the power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Trained Spotter reported reported 4 inches of snow in Tillamook. This was in addition to the around 4 inches of snow that fell on the previous day (February 6th).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was generally 1 to 4 inches of accumulating snow with the heaviest amounts south of downtown Portland. A CoCoRaHs observer reported 7 inches of snow in Greston, a NWS Employee reported 5.5 inches of snow in Oregon City, a person from the general public reported 4 inches in West Linn, and another NWS employee reported 3.8 inches of snow in SW Portland/Garden Home.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL COAST RANGE OF W OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 8 inches of snow fell in the Central Oregon Coast Range, gradually changing to ice in some of the valleys before ending. Someone from the general public reported 14 inches of new snow at Mary's Peak. Another person from the public reported 5 inches of snow in Alsea followed by light icing. A trained spotter reported 7 inches of snow in Blodgett followed by 0.5 inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Northwest Pacific combined with low pressure offshore resulted in gusty east winds through the Columbia River Gorge. Although the measured wind speeds were below wind advisory criteria, there were several reports of downed trees in the Portland Metro area that impacted travel, power supply, and general safety.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty east winds through the Columbia River Gorge resulted in impacts around the Portland metro area. Local media reported that a large tree fell onto a Max train and overhead wires in Gresham that closed that line for the rest of the day. Another downed tree crushed a SUV in Lake Oswego. A large tree took out power lines as it fell across Highway 43, disrupting power and closing the highway for a couple of hours. A Large tree branch fell and pierced through a window of a van that was driving on Highway 99 with luckily no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Preceding cold Arctic Air combined with a moist storm and strong East winds resulted in Blizzard conditions for the Western Columbia River Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Corbett was gusting greater than 35 mph with a peak gust to 60 mph. There were no snow reports in this zone, but near by reports suggests that there was 4 to 8 inches of snow. Cascade Locks reported 8 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow and ice produced 11.2 inches of snow 1.5 miles west of Timber. This snow was followed by 0.25 inch of ice. This wintery precipitation was in addition to the 10 to 12 inches of snow that fell on February 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell in the Central Willamette Valley. The precipitation changed to sleet briefly then to freezing rain late in the event. The Oregon State Police warned not to travel on Interstate 5 between Salem and Eugene due to the icy roadways. Some snow reports include 6 inches of snow in Salem, Turner and Aumsville. There was also 6 inches of snow in Dallas that was followed by 0.25 inch of ice. McMinnville measured 5 inches of snow, with Yamhill close behind at 4.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 9 inches of new snow fell in the Southern Willamette Valley. The snow eventually changed to sleet briefly, then freezing rain downing tree limbs and power lines. The Oregon State Police warned not to travel on Interstate 5 between Salem and Eugene due to the icy roadways. Power outages started to develop which continued to increase as the next event (Ice Storm Warning 1) developed on its heels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 8.5 inches of snow in Detroit. This is in addition to the 9 to 14 inches of snow that fell in the area on the previous day, February 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were few reports from the warning area but the Oregon State Police recommended against travel south of Eugene on Interstate 5 due to snow and ice. Snow eventually changed to ice, and power outages started to develop as the next event (Ice Storm Warning 1) developed. By 2300 PST, The Oregon Department of Transportation reported road closures near Cottage Grove due to downed trees and limbs from the weight of the ice. From nearby reports it is estimated that there was 4 to 8 inches of snow and 0.25 inch of ice.

There were also intermittent closures of Interstate 84 due to snow and icy roads.

A media meteorologist reported 0.5 inch of ice in his weather blog for Corbett. There were several reports from the Oregon Department of Transportation of Black Ice on the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A preceding cold arctic air mass combined with a moist Pacific storm resulted in widespread heavy snow for Northwest Oregon including the coast and the Willamette Valley. Warmer air quickly moved in from the south mid-morning and the snow changed to freezing rain.  East winds through the Columbia River Gorge maintained colder air to the north where the precipitation fell mainly as snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals varied across the Portland metro area ranging from 2 to 9 inches with the highest amounts near Hillsboro and Scappoose. A report from the general public of 8.5 inches of snow for Scappoose. There were two HAM Amateur radio reports of 6 inches of snow for Hillsboro and Gresham. Portland received 3 to 4 inches of snow which was reported from various sources including NWS employees, Trained spotters and the NWS office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf resulted in rapid rushes of water onto local beaches. One such surge of water swept two people into the ocean. A third person attempted to rescue one of the victims and was struck by a large log that was rolling in the tide. The injuries from this log were fatal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report on a social media network from the Oregon State Police News reported a fatality indirectly related to high surf at the North Jetty in Florence, OR. The time of the accident occurred at 5:55 pm which was around 3 hours after a High Surf Advisory had expired. Water rushing up the beach swept three family members that were sitting on a large log away from the log. One of the victims tried to help an elderly family member up from being knocked down by the water when the log estimated to be 40 ft long and 25 inches in diameter rolled over him. The man sustained fatal injuries from being struck by the log.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rock climber was hit by a large wave that swept him into the Ocean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was struck by a large wave while rock climbing at Cape Kiwanda in Pacific City, Oregon. The wave swept him into the ocean where he drowned. His body was found May 5, 2014 near Cape Lookout in Tillamook County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass and clearing skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing over some parts of south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures in this zone ranged from 27 to 37 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass and clearing skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing over some parts of south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported minimum temperature in this zone ranged from 21 to 32 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass and clearing skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing over some parts of south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures in this zone ranged from 30 to 33 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East winds brought very cold air from east of the Cascades through the Columbia River Gorge as a moist front pushed in from the Pacific. The combination of the cold air mass and frontal precipitation resulted in snow and ice for the Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of snow and ice in the Central Columbia River Gorge with generally 6 to 8 inches of snow. There was a quarter of an inch of ice on top of the snow in Hood River and White Salmon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A home in Woodburn, Oregon caught on Fire after being struck by lightning. There were also 2000 customers without power in Multnomah and Clackamas Counties from lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources reported that lightning struck a residence in Woodburn, OR on the Oregon Golf Association Golf Course causing a significant fire. The second floor of the home was completely destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought snow and ice to Northwest Oregon February 7th and 8th after a previous storm produced widespread heavy snow across the area on February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of snow and ice produced 3 inches of snow in Siletz and 0.25 inch of ice in Newport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass and clearing skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing over many areas in south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures in this zone ranged from 23 to 34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough combined with favorable upper level dynamics, mid-level instability and plenty of sub tropical moisture resulted in convective showers over the Portland metro area. Heavy rain from these showers resulted in significant flooding for the Beaverton/Hillsdale. Four homes, four apartments, the Winterhawks Skating Center, and a community center pool suffered damage from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from convective showers resulted in significant flooding near Beaverton. Flooding was reported of four homes, four apartments, several garages, the Winterhawks Skating Center, and a community pool. The city Public Works Director reported 2.3 inches of rain per hour, a personal weather station reported 2.29 inches of rain over 2 hours, and the National Weather Service Doppler radar estimated 1.7 inches per hour. A car stalled in a flooded high water in an underpass near the US 26-US 217 interchange. The cost damage is an estimate based on reports from the General Manager of the Skating Center and previous history of similar residential damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday, morning, August 6, 2014, ODF Incident Management Team 1 (Incident Commander John Buckman) was dispatched to the Rowena Fire, reported at approximately 9 p.m. PDT, Tuesday, August 5, burning six miles west of The Dalles. Joint command was established with the Oregon State Fire Marshal's Office, who dispatched OSFM IMT Green (Incident Commander John Ingrao) on this fire, and the Governor's Office also declared the fire a Conflagration Act on Wednesday morning, enabling firefighters and resources from other counties in Oregon to assist with structure protection on this fire.|| On Saturday, August 9, structure protection resources associated with the Oregon State Fire Marshal's Office were released from the Rowena Fire||FINAL INFORMATION FROM ODF IMT 1:|At 1 p.m. PDT on Tuesday, August 12, the Rowena Fire was 90 percent contained and ODF IMT 1 returned management of the fire to the ODF Central Oregon District - The Dalles Unit. A local fire team will continue work on this fire, finishing any needed mop-up to achieve 100 percent containment and control, as well as patrolling and monitoring it through the end of fire season.||This Inciweb Site includes the FINAL information from ODF IMT 1 on this fire (news release, maps, etc.) and is no longer being updated, nor will there be any further information posted to this site. The data in the Basic Information, listed below, is effective Tuesday, August 12, 2014, at 1500 hours (subsequent to transition to the local team) and is from the FINAL Incident Status Summary (ICS-209). ||As of August 12th: Mop-up continues on the 3,680-acre Rowena Fire, particularly on the west end above the homes on Highway 30 and in the area of McCall Point. Helicopters on Monday dropped buckets of water onto hot spots in the canyon below McCall Point; fire crews have limited access to this area, which is steep and dangerous due to rock slides and falling trees. | |Structures were threatened and some areas were evacuated. ||The fire is now 90% contained and command of the fire is being transferred to a Type 3 Incident Management Team (IC Burin) at 1:00 p.m. today. ODF���s Incident Command Team #1 assumed fire suppression responsibility for the fire Aug. 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: InciWeb information.

An upper level ridge combined with a surface thermal trough and low level offshore winds resulted in a hot day across Northwest Oregon where inland temperatures peaked in the upper 90s inland and the upper 80s along the coast. The NWS Portland office had a heat advisory in effect. Many people flocked to the rivers to cool from the heat and there was unfortunately one known drowning at Wildwood Falls near Cottage Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boy slipped on rocks at the Wildwood Falls near Cottage Grove, and slid around 30 feet on rocks into the water. His older brother went into the water to save the boy and both got sucked down a narrow chute of water. The younger brother survived, but the older one did not. His body was later found trapped under a large log under the chute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of fronts resulted in a prolonged period of rain for Northwest Oregon, and minor flooding of several of the area's rivers from February 12th through February 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Siuslaw River near Mapelton reached flood stage at 440 PM PST on February 12, 2014, and crested near 18.02 feet at 500 PM PST. The river dropped below flood stage at 530 PM PST on February 12th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mohawk River near Springfield reached flood stage at 400 PM PST. The river crested at 15.1 feet at 515 PM PST, and fell below flood level at 630 PM PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front produced heavy rain over Northwest Oregon which resulted in the flooding of eight rivers. Another impact from the rain were a couple of land/rock slides that both blocked two highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Siletz River at Siletz reached flood stage of 20 feet at 1045 PM PST on December 20th. The river crested at 20.33 feet at 1230 AM PST on December 21st, before dropping below flood stage an hour later.
